unit.name: Instruction fine-tuning and evaluation
unit.description: Covers supervised instruction tuning over many tasks, benchmark caveats, and data scaling lessons.
unit.summary: Instruction fine-tuning is introduced because pretraining predicts the next token rather than directly assisting users [ts=1352s] [ts=1428s]. The recipe is to collect many instruction-output pairs across tasks such as question answering, summarization, translation, and chain-of-thought reasoning, train on those pairs, and evaluate on unseen tasks [ts=1461s] [ts=1488s]. The instructor emphasizes data plus scale: modern instruction tuning spans thousands of tasks and millions of examples [ts=1499s] [ts=1519s]. Evaluation is difficult; MMLU is presented as a broad multiple-choice benchmark, but the lecture warns about leakage, ambiguity, benchmark saturation, and limited utility [ts=1536s] [ts=1727s]. FLAN-T5 results show that larger models become more responsive to instruction tuning, with gains increasing as parameter count rises [ts=1736s] [ts=1777s]. The section also notes that stronger models can generate instruction data for smaller ones, and that high-quality examples may reduce the required number of examples [ts=1844s] [ts=1885s].

key_points.text: Instruction fine-tuning trains on instruction-output pairs across many tasks.
Instruction tuning has scaled from one task to thousands of tasks and millions of examples.
MMLU is used as a broad benchmark over diverse knowledge questions.
Evaluation is tricky because of leakage, ambiguity, and benchmark saturation.
Larger FLAN-T5 models benefit more from instruction tuning than smaller models.
Strong LLMs can generate instruction-output pairs for training smaller models.

unit_summary_for_generation: Instruction fine-tuning is introduced because pretraining predicts the next token rather than directly assisting users [ts=1352s] [ts=1428s]. The recipe is to collect many instruction-output pairs across tasks such as question answering, summarization, translation, and chain-of-thought reasoning, train on those pairs, and evaluate on unseen tasks [ts=1461s] [ts=1488s]. The instructor emphasizes data plus scale: modern instruction tuning spans thousands of tasks and millions of examples [ts=1499s] [ts=1519s]. Evaluation is difficult; MMLU is presented as a broad multiple-choice benchmark, but the lecture warns about leakage, ambiguity, benchmark saturation, and limited utility [ts=1536s] [ts=1727s]. FLAN-T5 results show that larger models become more responsive to instruction tuning, with gains increasing as parameter count rises [ts=1736s] [ts=1777s]. The section also notes that stronger models can generate instruction data for smaller ones, and that high-quality examples may reduce the required number of examples [ts=1844s] [ts=1885s].

kp_catalog.name: Instruction fine-tuning and alignment vocabulary
kp_catalog.description: Definitions of instruction fine-tuning, supervised fine-tuning, alignment, and preference fine-tuning. Supervised training on instruction-output pairs so models follow user intent.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because instruction fine-tuning and alignment vocabulary formalizes how definitions of instruction fine-tuning, supervised fine-tuning, alignment, and preference fine-tuning, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context though importance labels split between critical and high.

kp_catalog.name: LLM evaluation and instruction-data scaling
kp_catalog.description: Benchmarks such as MMLU, benchmark caveats, and scaling effects in instruction tuning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because lLM evaluation and instruction-data scaling clarifies benchmarks such as MMLU, benchmark caveats, and scaling effects in instruction tuning, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
kp_catalog.description: A prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting. Prompting with worked intermediate reasoning steps so the model generates a scratchpad before the final answer.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chain-of-thought prompting formalizes how a prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between capstone and gateway and importance labels split between critical and high.
